content.problemStatement:
You are given an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] containing **distinct positive** integers.

Let BBB be a permutation of AAA. Define the value of BBB to be
∑i=1N(Bi mod Bi+1)\sum_{i=1}^N (B_i \bmod{B_{i+1}})∑i=1N​(Bi​modBi+1​)

where BN+1B_{N+1}BN+1​ is treated to be B1B_1B1​.

Find the **maximum** value across all permutations of AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers — the elements of AAA.


Output Format

For each test case, output a new line containing the answer — the maximum possible value across all permutations of AAA.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^3
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 2000) – the size of the array. The second line contains N distinct positive integers A1, A2, …, AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^15).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible value of Σ_{i=1}^{N} (B_i mod B_{i+1}) over all permutations B of the given array, where B_{N+1}=B_1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: sum=12, max=7, min=2 → answer = 12‑7 + (7%2)=5+1=6, which is achieved by the permutation [2,3,7].

solution.approach:
For any two distinct numbers x < y we have x mod y = x and y mod x = y % x < x. To maximise each term B_i mod B_{i+1} we would like B_i < B_{i+1} because then the term equals B_i, the larger possible value for that position. Therefore we can arrange the numbers in strictly increasing order. All N‑1 edges of the circle are then ascending and contribute the smaller element of each adjacent pair, i.e. all elements except the largest one. The remaining edge is from the largest element back to the smallest; its contribution is max % min. No other arrangement can give a larger sum because any descending edge replaces a term equal to the smaller element by a remainder that is strictly smaller than that element. Hence the optimal value is:
    answer = (sum of all elements) – maxElement + (maxElement % minElement).
The algorithm for each test case is therefore:
1. Scan the array, simultaneously compute the total sum, the minimum and the maximum.
2. Compute answer using the formula above.
3. Output the answer.
The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over the whole input.
